synopsis: Ryan Holiday exposes the mechanics by which online rumors are manufactured and traded up through the media ecosystem until they become mainstream headlines with real-world consequences. Drawing on his own experience as a media manipulator, he details how bloggers and marketers exploit the toxic economics of digital news to spread misinformation at scale.

sources.title: Trust Me, I'm Lying – Penguin Random House

synopsis: Professor Pierre Aronnax and two companions, after investigating reports of a sea monster, are captured and held aboard the futuristic submarine Nautilus by its enigmatic commander, Captain Nemo. The novel follows their globe-spanning underwater journey through oceans, lost ruins, and polar ice as they seek an opportunity to escape.

sources.title: Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea – Project Gutenberg

synopsis: Scott Young examines a self-directed approach to learning he calls ultralearning, in which individuals design intense, focused projects to master difficult skills rapidly without formal instruction. Drawing on historical examples and current cognitive research, the book presents nine principles for structuring such projects to achieve deep, lasting competence.

Set over a single day in Dublin on June 16, 1904, the novel follows advertising canvasser Leopold Bloom and young writer Stephen Dedalus as their paths converge across the city in a loose parallel to Homer's Odyssey. James Joyce structures the narrative across eighteen episodes, each experimenting with a distinct prose style to render the interior lives of its characters.

Three astronauts aboard the spacecraft Cetus Prime travel to Mars to investigate the mysterious destruction of three probes, suspected to have been caused by UMOs—unidentified magnetic objects that behave as electromagnetic life forms. The novella serves as a prequel to the Rorschach Explorer Missions series and centers on the crew's attempt to gather evidence without triggering a lethal swarm.

sources.title: UMO – K. Patrick Donoghue official site

synopsis: Vickie Schumacher explains how a revocable living trust works as an alternative to a will, outlining how it enables individuals to avoid probate, retain control of assets during their lifetime, and simplify the transfer of wealth at death. The book walks through how to create and fund a trust, what supplemental documents are needed, and how to tailor an estate plan to individual circumstances.

Marshall McLuhan argues that the nature of a medium rather than its content shapes human perception and social organization, a premise he calls the medium is the message. The book surveys media from print to television, treating each as an extension of human faculties whose structural form alters how people think and relate.

Sasha Pachev, a former MySQL development team member, provides a technical tour of MySQL's internal architecture, covering its core data structures, storage engine interface, and replication system. The book is designed for developers who want to understand how MySQL works at the code level in order to extend, tune, or contribute to the database.

Law professor Adam Benforado contends that the failures of the American criminal justice system stem not from individual misconduct but from cognitive biases hardwired into all human minds. Using psychology and neuroscience research alongside historical cases, the book traces how unconscious forces distort police investigations, witness identification, jury deliberations, and sentencing, and proposes structural reforms to address them.

Anne-Marie Slaughter argues that true gender equality requires restructuring workplaces and revaluing caregiving rather than asking individuals to manage incompatible demands on their own. Drawing on her experience leaving a senior government role and on broader research, she proposes that when men and women share caregiving equally, both individuals and economies benefit.

Raj Shah and Christopher Kirchhoff recount the founding and operations of the Pentagon's Defense Innovation Unit, an organization created to bring commercial technology from Silicon Valley into the U.S. military. The book traces how the unit worked to accelerate the adoption of artificial intelligence, autonomous systems, and other emerging technologies to transform how modern warfare is conducted.

Stuart Milton wakes in a strange house with no memory of how he arrived, his pregnant wife missing and his claims dismissed by everyone around him, and is placed under the care of a psychiatrist. In parallel, a team of Oxford scientists develops secret technology that draws Stuart unwittingly into a web of espionage, betrayal, and murder.

In an alternate history where a failed Apollo 8 mission shattered the celestial sphere and made divine Names literally powerful, kabbalists, angels, and corporations wage war over sacred words that can reshape reality. The novel tracks an eclectic cast—including Silicon Valley billionaires seeking to confront God and a Las Vegas dark lord—as their schemes converge on the apocalypse.

Logan Ramsay discovers his genome has been altered, his cognitive and physical capabilities steadily amplifying in ways tied to a dark family legacy and a plan to reengineer the human species. With his enhanced abilities making him the only one who can stop the scheme, he must decide how far he is willing to transform himself.

Anderson Cooper and Katherine Howe trace the Vanderbilt family from Cornelius Vanderbilt's accumulation of a vast fortune in shipping and railroads through successive generations who expanded, squandered, and ultimately dispersed the wealth. The book examines how the family shaped the Gilded Age economy and culture while chronicling the personal rivalries and extravagances that marked their decline.

Tarang Shah and Shital Shah present structured interviews with thirty-five prominent venture capitalists, asking each about the criteria they use to evaluate investments, how they structure deals, and the common mistakes that cause startups to fail. The book draws on conversations with investors who backed companies including YouTube, Twitter, Facebook, and Zappos to illustrate how top VCs identify and build high-growth businesses.

Brad Feld and Jason Mendelson break down every clause of a venture capital term sheet, explaining what each provision means economically and how it affects founder control, so entrepreneurs can negotiate with informed confidence. The book covers the full funding process, from understanding who the key players are to recognizing the legal and strategic implications of deal terms.

Amy Herman adapts the curriculum of her Art of Perception seminar, which uses close observation of artworks to train professionals—including police officers, physicians, and military personnel—to see more accurately and communicate what they notice. The book applies these visual analysis techniques to real-world decision-making, showing how disciplined looking can sharpen judgment across a wide range of fields.

sources.title: Visual Intelligence – Amy Herman official site

synopsis: Neal Gabler traces Walt Disney's life from a childhood of hardship through his decades of innovation in animation, film, television, and theme parks, drawing on complete access to the Disney archives. The biography portrays Disney as a complex figure whose creative ambitions were shadowed by financial crises, periods of withdrawal, and feelings of inadequacy.
